Start Your Project
How I work

I make the shape of the work clear before the build gets expensive.

Most engineering pain starts earlier than the code. It starts when scope is vague, risks are hidden, or no one has agreed on what actually needs to work. My process is designed to make those things visible early.

Solo by design

You speak directly to the person doing the architecture, implementation, and technical tradeoff decisions.

Clear boundaries

I define what is in scope, what needs hardening, and what can wait so the work does not collapse into vagueness.

Step 1 We agree on exactly what needs to work.

I identify the people, systems, and state transitions involved before reaching for implementation detail.

Step 2 I design the system around where things could go wrong.

If timing, reconciliation, or auditability can damage trust later, those concerns become first-class design inputs.

Step 3 I ship in small, visible pieces so you're never in the dark.

That keeps the system understandable and makes it easier to catch drift, hidden assumptions, and unnecessary complexity.

Step 4 I finish by testing the hard edges, not just the happy path.

I care about how the product behaves when a dependency is slow, a value is stale, or an operator needs a straight answer.

Engineering stance

The system still needs to make sense when the easy path breaks.

These are the ideas underneath the process. They are how I keep a build readable, dependable, and easier to recover when something goes sideways.

One source of truth

When several tools touch the same state, I establish authority early so the system can reconcile instead of arguing with itself.

Operator readability

I expose the information that matters to the person running the system, not just the person who built it.

Recoverable behavior

Failures should produce understandable states and reasonable next actions, not mystery or panic.

SS Developers
